blocks.text: Compiling the revelations in a record book was the next step in preparing them for publication. Why Joseph Smith and early Church leaders chose to take this step is not clear, but they may have been following a system of record keeping that was customary in the United States of America. When people bought land, for example, they received a document—often called a deed—that proved they now owned the property. They took the deed to a recorder’s office, where it was entered on the government’s record books.

Joseph Smith had experienced a similar record-keeping procedure when he registered the copyright of the Book of Mormon on June 11, 1829. On that day, he or his representative took a copy of the book’s title page into his local federal district court, where a clerk gave him a copyright certificate and entered a copy of the document in a bound record book for future reference. Joseph kept the certificate as a personal record.

The Church adopted a similar process for Joseph Smith’s revelations. Once the revelations were copied from the original loose sheets into the Church’s official revelation book, the original sheets might be retained by those to whom they were directed or possibly even discarded as no longer needed.

Not long after the Church was organized, Joseph Smith began working with John Whitmer to arrange the loose sheets of revelations and eventually copy them into a bound manuscript volume that came to be known as the “Book of Commandments and Revelations.” Because many of the original dictation copies of the revelations did not survive, the Book of Commandments and Revelations contains the earliest copies of many of the revelations that were later published in the Doctrine and Covenants. It is therefore one of the Church’s most precious and important manuscripts.

Besides John Whitmer, Oliver Cowdery also helped copy revelations into the Book of Commandments and Revelations. Joseph Smith, John Whitmer, Oliver Cowdery, Sidney Rigdon, and William W. Phelps all helped to review and correct the manuscript for publication.

Key decisions about publishing Joseph Smith’s revelations came at a series of Church conferences held in early November 1831. In the first months after the Church was organized in April 1830, the number of Church members and investigators was small, and revelations were generally shared through handwritten copies. But by the end of 1831, there were hundreds of baptized members as well as others who were curious to learn the doctrines contained in the revelations of the Prophet.

The conference that convened November 1, 1831, in Hiram, Ohio, optimistically voted to publish ten thousand copies of Joseph’s revelations in book form. On July 20 of that year, Joseph Smith had received a revelation while in Independence, Jackson County, Missouri, declaring that area to be “the land of promise, and the place for the city of Zion.” The revelation also directed that William W. Phelps, a recent convert and newspaperman, travel to Missouri “and be established as a printer unto the church.”

Oliver Cowdery was soon assigned to carry the Book of Commandments and Revelations to Missouri so the revelations could be printed there. He was also directed to take funds needed in Missouri. A November 1831 revelation supplemented this plan: “Hearken unto me, saith the Lord your God, for my servant Oliver Cowdery’s sake,” the revelation began. “It is not wisdom in me that he should be entrusted with the commandments and the moneys which he shall carry unto the land of Zion, except one go with him who will be true and faithful. Wherefore, I, the Lord, will that my servant, John Whitmer, should go with my servant Oliver Cowdery.”

Once Oliver and John left for Missouri with the Book of Commandments and Revelations, another official record book was needed into which Joseph Smith’s revelations could be copied. On February 16, 1832, in Hiram, Ohio, while working on his new translation of the Bible, Joseph had a remarkable vision that was also witnessed by Sidney Rigdon. “And while we were yet in the Spirit,” they recalled, “the Lord commanded us that we should write the vision.” They did so, and it eventually became known as Doctrine and Covenants 76, though in the days before the revelations were published in book form, it was simply called “The Vision” by Church members.

Not long after the vision was first recorded, it was copied into a new record book consistent with the pattern that had been followed up to that point. The front cover of the new record book was labeled “Book of Revelations,” and at some point a paper label was added to the spine reading “Kirtland Revelations.” As a result, it became known as the Kirtland Revelation Book.

In addition to the two official record books of revelations—the Book of Commandments and Revelations and the Kirtland Revelation Book—a few other manuscript revelation books were developed during the early history of the Church. These were compilations made by individuals who wanted access to copies of the revelations before they were generally available to the public in printed book form.

An example of such manuscript revelation books is the “Book of Commandments, Laws, and Covenants” kept by Orson Hyde and Samuel H. Smith, brother of Joseph Smith, during a mission to which they were called in a revelation dated January 25, 1832.

Samuel Smith made an entry in his journal on November 16, 1832, while he and his missionary companion were in the state of Maine. According to his journal, they stopped that day in part because they wanted to record revelations to which they had access. The book into which they copied these revelations has survived and today is part of the collections of the Church History Library in Salt Lake City, Utah.
Others also compiled manuscript revelation books for their own use until published copies of revelations became readily accessible to them.
